guideline_references: ASGE Guideline: management of ingested foreign bodies and food impactions in the GI tract (2011)
ESGE Guideline: management of ingested foreign bodies and food bolus impaction (2016)
AGA / ACG clinical guideline: diagnosis and management of eosinophilic oesophagitis
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present after-hours clinician who inspected the oropharynx, auscultated the chest, attempted effervescent-granule dislodgement and facilitated ED transfer for emergency endoscopic disimpaction. The AI Doctor CANNOT examine, cannot attempt disimpaction, and cannot perform endoscopy — it is scored on recognising an acute complete oesophageal food-bolus obstruction, arranging urgent ED endoscopy, warning NIL BY MOUTH (do not drink water to wash it down), and escalating to 000 on airway/perforation features.

Chronic-on-acute: the underlying suspected eosinophilic oesophagitis is a chronic condition normally low-tier, but this presents WITH an acute complication — complete food-bolus impaction with inability to clear secretions (drooling) — so the case is tiered to the complication (ED-now emergency), not the chronic label.

Answer-key discipline: the source note prints the diagnosis, a 'textbook demonstration ... stacked circular rings and eosinophilic infiltration on biopsy strongly suspected' triad-match line, a ruled-out differential screen (peptic stricture, achalasia, malignancy) and an anticipated confirmatory endoscopy/biopsy result up front — all sealed in nodes 10/11 and absent from 01/02.

Commission trap: the classic wrong move is telling the patient to drink water/fizzy drink to force the bolus down — dangerous in complete obstruction (regurgitation and aspiration). Encoded as an error of commission.

Age (~35) and sex (male) inferred from a 75 kg adult with a partner, a year of solid-food dysphagia, PPI-refractory reflux and an atopic history (EoE demographics skew young/middle-aged male) — flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: GIH (Gastroenterology & Hepatology), source case GIH-019.
